Carbon dots (CDs) are new members of carbon-based nanomaterials that can be used in cancer treatment to reduce side effects of drugs and improve treatment efficiency, offering new medical opportunities for further research. Urged as such, we are encouraged to classify CDs-based cancer treatments, including photodynamic therapy (PDT), photothermal therapy (PTT), sonodynamic therapy (SDT), chemodynamic therapy (CDT), chemotherapy (CT), and synergistic therapy. The advantages of these approaches are summarized, as well as ways to improve certain shortcomings. Meanwhile, this review highlights the feasible practice of CDs in cancer treatment, which is further developed and widely used in the biomedical field.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Illustration of multifunctional CDs for tumor theranostics.

With advances in nanotechnology and the development of emerging therapeutic modalities, a surge in research on the use of nanomedicines for biomedical applications has occurred over the past three decades. Carbon dots (CDs) are new members of carbon-based nanomaterials that can be used in cancer treatment to reduce side effects of drugs and improve treatment efficiency, offering new medical opportunities for further research. Urged as such, we are encouraged to classify CDs-based cancer treatments, including photodynamic therapy (PDT), photothermal therapy (PTT), sonodynamic therapy (SDT), chemodynamic therapy (CDT), chemotherapy (CT), and synergistic therapy. The advantages of these approaches are summarized, as well as ways to improve certain shortcomings. Meanwhile, this review highlights the feasible practice of CDs in cancer treatment, which is further developed and widely used in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a group of chronic diseases that seriously endanger human health, accounting for about 9.6 million deaths worldwide each year. 1–3 The main methods currently used for clinical cancer treatment are surgery, chemotherapy (CT), radiotherapy and immunotherapy. 4–7 These treatments still have a number of side effects in practice, including drug resistance, imbalance of the immune system and triggering post-operative complications, 8–11 which seriously affect the outcome of patients' recovery. Although research into cancer has yielded some results in recent years, the detection and eradication of malignant tumors remain a challenge. Accordingly, several new techniques have been developed to treat cancer accurately and efficiently.

Cancer treatments that have been developed and improved include monotherapy and synergistic therapy. Monotherapies include photodynamic therapy (PDT), photothermal therapy (PTT), sonodynamic therapy (SDT), chemodynamic therapy (CDT), and CT. Synergistic therapy is a combination of these monotherapies that work together. In PDT, the photosensitizer activated by the corresponding light transfers its excited state energy to the surrounding oxygen, which generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) thus leading to apoptosis of tumor cells. 12–17 PTT utilises photothermal agents (PTAs) to absorb photon energy and raise the temperature above 40 °C for the purpose of ablating tumors. 18–20 To date, both pyrolysis and sonoluminescence mechanisms have been used to describe the SDT process. 21–25 The cavitation effect occurs under the excitation of ultrasound, and eventually the energy generated by the bubble rupture causes the pyrolysis of water to
OH. In addition, the bubble rupture around the sonosensitizers creates sonoluminescence, which excites the sonosensitizers to produce ROS. 26–28 Through the catalytic effect of transition metals in CDT reagents, H2O2 in tumors produces
OH through the Fenton reaction that acts on biomolecules such as DNA and proteins in cells, thereby inducing apoptosis. 29–34 CT is one of the main clinical treatments in which anti-cancer drugs are taken orally or by injection to a systemic level to kill tumour cells. 35–37 However, the above-mentioned monotherapeutic approaches lead to limited efficiency in the elimination of malignant tumors due to their inherent drawbacks. The low tissue penetration of light reduces the efficiency of phototherapy for deep lesions and the retention time of PSs after administration can have side effects reducing the further use of PDT. 38–40 Overexpression of heat shock proteins and damage to normal tissue caused by overheating of the tumour area limit the effectiveness of PTT. 41–43 The lack of ability of the sonosensitizers to produce ROS in SDT and the dispersion of ultrasound also cause suboptimal selectivity and damage to normal tissue. 44 The main reasons limiting CDT are the effects of TME: weak acidity does not trigger most Fenton reactions; the insufficient concentration of H2O2 weakens the catalytic efficiency; and overexpression of reducing substances counteracts ROS production. 45–47 Conventional CT is often associated with serious side effects such as alopecia, anaemia and systemic toxicity. 48–50 Combining the above approaches in a synergistic manner can alleviate the inherent shortcomings of single therapies, thus creating a “1 + 1 > 2” effect and facilitating the development of cancer treatment approaches. For example, in PDT/PTT, the ROS produced by PDT inhibits heat shock proteins 51-53 and improves the effect of PTT, while the heat generated by PTT increases intravascular oxygen levels and improves the hypoxic environment of the tumor in PDT. The generation of multiple ROS species within tumor cells can enhance therapeutic efficacy and therefore SDT/CDT synergy can be used to treat deep-seated tumors with superior tumor killing ability.

The research on carbon dots (CDs) for cancer treatment has made great progress. CDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with excellent biocompatibility, facile functionalization, and stable photoluminescence. 54–56 With a similar large π-conjugation system to organic molecules, the core is mainly graphitised sp2 carbon. The shell layer contains abundant functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups, which have excellent water solubility and facilitate binding to therapeutic reagents. 57–59 The covalent carbon skeleton structure also enhances the stability of the CDs and allows for safe residence in the human body. 60 Their size is similar to that of proteins, DNA, ion channels and glomerular filtration barriers in vivo, 18 which allows them to be ideal vehicles for precise therapeutic treatment under tumor microenvironment (TME) stimulation. In addition, the stable optical signal and red to near infrared absorption properties enable CDs to have a high penetration depth in tissues for visualisation of tumor therapy. In PDT, CDs improve the water solubility of conventional photosensitizers (PSs) and some CDs also have the ability to convert light energy into ROS, which can be used as PSs alone to enhance therapeutic effects. The sp2 and sp3 atoms as well as π electrons in CDs significantly increase defects and heteroatoms in the photoactive system in PTT, 61–63 the absorbed light energy is converted into heat that ablates tumor cells. The shell of CDs is subjected to cavitation bubbles in SDT, which destroy groups on the surface of CDs and react rapidly enough with the surrounding environment to generate ROS. 64–65 In CDT and CT, the large specific surface area of CDs facilitates the loading of metal cations and drugs. 66–68 These metal cations can be detected by fluorescence enhancement or bursting to monitor the Fenton reaction and glutathione (GSH) in TME. The above advantages pave the way for the use of CDs-based nanodrugs in clinical oncology therapy. Furthermore, combining the above approaches can improve the inherent shortcomings of a single approach and facilitate the development of new therapeutic approaches. The use of CDs for cancer treatment is currently attracting widespread interest, and unfortunately the review of approaches to CDs for cancer treatment is scarce.

The use of CDs for the treatment of cancer is currently of wide interest, while reviews of CDs treatments are scarce and focus mainly on a single approach. This review presents the applications based on CDs for cancer treatments in the past five years, mainly divided into PDT, PTT, SDT, CDT, CT and synergistic therapies including PDT/PTT, PTT/CDT, SDT/CDT, PDT/CT, PTT/CT, CDT/CT, PDT/CDT/CT, PDT/PTT/CDT and PDT/PTT/CT. The advantages of CDs in monotherapy and synergistic therapy are summarised, as well as ways to improve certain shortcomings. The aim of this review is to highlight the importance of CDs-based bioimaging and cancer treatments, and to present the current research directions in therapeutic approaches in order to inform clinical treatment strategies for CDs and realize the potential for efficient treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The main methods of CDs for cancer therapy
Monocular methods of CDs for cancer therapy
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are promising tools in cancer treatment. The main approach is to use the introduction of anti-cancer drugs, genes or therapeutic intermediates into the diseased tissue to achieve tumor eradication. CDs have a large specific surface area and can load these drugs into cells through endocytosis or direct translocation and release them in TME or under laser and ultrasound stimulation. 69 In addition, through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, anti-cancer drugs can be selectively focused on tumor sites, which makes drug treatment more efficient. This section describes the progress of CDs in cancer therapies including PDT, PTT, SDT, CDT and CT.

CDs generally participate in PDT as PSs or carriers of PSs. The abundance of functional groups allows conventional photosensitive molecules to be effectively loaded onto the surface of CDs through covalent coupling or supramolecular interactions (including electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking), to improve therapeutic efficacy. 70 As shown in Fig. 1 , CDs can destroy tumor cells by generating ROS through the excitation of peripheral oxygen by light of the corresponding wavelength. The crossover transition from S1 to T1 state under light and the energy transfer from T1 to S0 state via PSs ensures a yield of 1O2 greater than 1. 18 The light sources, photosensitive molecules and molecular oxygen present in the tissues that generate ROS are improved by elemental doping and coupled assembly of CDs for the purpose of enhancing the effect of PDT.
(1)
Element-doped CDs for photodynamic therapy

By doping with heteroatoms, CDs can be purposefully modified. The addition of these elements enhances NIR absorption, tumor targeting and cellular imaging, improves the electron donation/acceptance capacity of CDs, promotes redox reaction on the surface of CDs and exhibits excellent therapeutic effects. 71 The introduction of transition metals as active centres into CDs can improve the photodynamic therapeutic effect of the material. Bi et al. 72 prepared copper-doped CDs-1 in Fig. 2 using acrylic acid and copper nitrate as precursors. CDs-1 act as PSs to convert surrounding oxygen molecules to 1O2 under light, which results in cytotoxicity and bright green fluorescence. The quantum yield (QY) of 1O2 was 36 %. CDs-1 can inhibit HeLa cells and SH-SY5Y, and their fluorescence enables tumor imaging, which has promising applications in the direction of light-guided PDT.

PDT is limited due to its inherent defects. The short lifespan of ROS that are generated within the tumor site thus leads to limited propagation distance and reduced activity in killing tumor cells, 73–75 which affects the therapeutic effect. Generating ROS within the nucleus of tumor cells, directly inhibiting key organelles and causing cell death, can maximize the efficiency of ROS utilization and effectively overcome the above problems.

The TME limits the activity of ROS, so subjecting CDs to PDT in key tumor organelles can overcome this restriction. Xu et al. 76 prepared selenium/nitrogen doped CDs-2 by a one-step solvothermal method, as shown in Fig. 3 . CDs-2 bound to RNA were transported near the nucleus of MCF-7 cells to produce ROS and disrupt the nuclear membrane under light, allowing more CDs-2 to enter and therefore increasing the efficiency of tumor killing. The efficiency of producing 1O2 by this method was 10.6 %. It is a unique nuclear targeting property of CDs-2 that overcomes the problems of short ROS lifespan and limited diffusion distance, thus effectively inhibiting MCF-7 growth.

Most CDs have blue photoluminescence properties and their applications in the biological field are limited. Blue and green emissions cannot penetrate effectively into tumor cells, which may lead to automatic fluorescence of biological samples, and the emission efficiency is lower than that of traditional quantum dots. 77–78 CDs with bright fluorescence emission in the red/NIR region can penetrate tissues more efficiently, have high photobleaching resistance, strong absorption and biodegradation in the red/NIR region. 79 .

Red-emitting CDs have a high penetration depth into tissues and have excellent tumour killing ability. Zhao et al. 80 prepared nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs-3 from o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid, as shown in Fig. 4 . The absolute fluorescence QY of CDs-3 was 15 %. In the presence of oxygen, more 1O2 can be produced by illumination to improve the PDT effect. Similarly, Yue et al. 81 prepared ruthenium-containing CDs-4 by a hydrothermal method. The DNA photolysis ability suggested that plasmid DNA may be destroyed by CDs-4 and light. The maximum emission peak occurred at 606 nm, the photoluminescence lifetime was 0.65 µs, and the fluorescence QY was 20.79 %. CDs-3 and CDs-4 emitting red light reduce the photodamage to biological tissues, they hold great promise for the use of red light CDs in cancer therapy.

Elemental doping can lead to CDs with tunable structural defects and enhanced optical properties, with new surface states promoting radiative recombination and higher fluorescence quantum yields. 82–84 In addition to this non-covalent and covalent coupling strategies can be used to further tune the functional properties of CDs. Non-covalent strategies typically refer to modification methods that utilise non-specific forces such as electrostatic interactions, chelation, hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking. 85 Covalent coupling strategies typically involve the formation of covalent bonds via the abundant surface functional groups on CDs to prepare multifunctional therapeutic reagents with improved biocompatibility. 86 The potential of modified CDs for tumour therapy is greatly enhanced.
(2)
Coupling and assembly of CDs for photodynamic therapy

Natural biomass CDs are less biotoxic and reduce the risk of harm to humans. Xue et al. 87 prepared a natural biomass CDs-5 from lychee peel. As shown in Fig. 5 , the nanoprobe consisted of CDs-5 as a PSs carrier and an imaging light source, Tf as a navigation molecule, and chlorine e6 (Ce6) was used as a fluorescent receptor for PSs. Induction into Bel-7404 cells by Tf-mediated endocytosis and fluorescence imaging by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs-5 and Ce6. Under 650 nm light irradiation, Ce6 on CDs-5 surface released 1O2 molecule through PDT to kill Bel-7404 cells. Ce6 and Tf are assembled onto natural biomass CDs to improve the therapeutic effect of CDs-5, which have the advantages of less toxicity to cells, green and easy access, with broad application prospects in the biomedical field.

The imbalance between the supply and consumption of oxygen causes tumor hypoxia. As tumor proliferation consumes oxygen, it affects the diffusion of oxygen into the deep-seated tumor and limits the efficiency of PDT. 88–90 The rapid oxygen consumption during PDT makes the hypoxic situation within the TME more severe, which greatly limits the therapeutic effect of CDs.

The hypoxic environment within the tumor limits the production of 1O2, which leads to the recurrence of cancer. To mitigate the hypoxic environment in tumors, Jia et al. 91 prepared CDs-6 with magnetic fluorescence using manganese(II) phthalocyanine as a precursor. The synergistic self-assembly of CDs-6 and DPE-PEG improved their water solubility and biocompatibility. As shown in Fig. 6 , the assemblies of CDs-6 could not only efficiently generate 1O2 with a QY of 40 %, but also highly catalyze the production of oxygen from H2O2 for dual-mode fluorescence imaging (FLI)/magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). CDs-6 improve the hypoxic environment of tumors and overcome the inherent disadvantages of PDT, this work provides a promising tool for cancer therapy.

PDT has a limited ability to penetrate tissue, which affects the effectiveness of the treatment. To address this issue, Shen et al.
92 prepared CDs-7 using carbonised polymer dots self-assembled into aggregates in water, followed by self-assembly with amphiphilic polymers and chemiluminescent donors (CPPO) in Fig. 7 . The reaction of H2O2 in A549 cells with CPPO produced intermediates that can transfer luminescence by electron exchange with the carbonised polymer dots, enabling chemiluminescent imaging. The luminescence time exceeded 40 min. The H2O2 depletion process also generated cytotoxic ROS that effectively killed tumor cells. Using own chemiluminescence as a light source, CDs-7 increase the penetration depth of PDT, which open up new avenues for the application of CDs in clinical tumor therapy.
(3)
Other methods of modifying CDs for photodynamic therapy

The excellent water solubility of CDs can enhance the PDT of conventional PSs. Yue et al. 93 used riboflavin as carbon source and ethylenediamine as crosslinking agent to prepare CDs-8 by conventional one-step hydrothermal method. The optical properties of VB2 were partially retained, CDs-8 water solubility and fluorescence quantum yield were greatly improved. As shown in Fig. 8 , CDs-8 can easily enter cells through endocytosis and stimulate O2 to 1O2 under irradiation, so as to achieve antitumor effect. He et al.
94 synthesized water-soluble fluorescent CDs-9. 1O2 was generated in the lysosome with a single laser irradiation at 540 nm and the QY of 1O2 was about 27.6 %. CDs can improve the aqueous solubility of PSs as a strategy for achieving ideal tumor therapy.

Optimisation of chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) between light and CDs to improve the utilisation of effectively excited light. Kun et al. 95 designed CDs nanocarriers, chemiluminescence and PSs Ce6 to form an efficient unified system (CDs-10) in Fig. 9 . The CDs-10 system was successful in vitro in generating ROS with a yield of 35.93 % and killed about 90 % of SMMC-7721 cells. Its enhanced therapeutic effect is attributed to two ways: one is the direct CRET process, the other is the CRET process and the subsequent CDs-10 mediated FRET. The CDs-10-Ce6 system improves the limited penetration depth in conventional PDT which shows excellent promise in the direction of deep-seated tumor therapy.

In addition to the above methods, CDs can also facilitate the treatment of deep-seated tumors through two-photon excitation. Nasrin et al. 96 synthesized a novel conjugated CDs (CDs-11) as two-photon active PSs using a combination of folic acid and curcumin in Fig. 10 . CDs-11 were used for targeting PDT by directly attacking DNA in the nucleus and releasing lethal ROS. Two-photon photon excitation enhanced the production of ROS in the nucleus. CDs-11 utilize two-photon excited PDT with self-targeting and ROS generation capabilities as well, which is a new avenue for cancer therapy.

CDs can effectively improve the water solubility and biocompatibility of PSs as well as enhance the effect of PDT by targeting tumor tissues. The stability of CDs loaded PSs can be affected by the complex environment in the organism, and the physical and chemical properties of PSs can also affect CDs. Table 1 summaries the synthesis of various precursors, methods, excitation wavelengths and yields of 1O2 for different CDs. Due to the limited penetration depth of PDT, only superficial tumors can be treated and the short propagation distance of the generated ROS affects the therapeutic efficacy. CDs-12 with a longer emission wavelength and the addition of a targeting factor overcomes these drawbacks that enhance the effectiveness of PDT.

CDs can have applications as carriers and PTAs in PTT. When they act as carriers controlled release of PTAs can be achieved by TME stimulation. CDs are considered attractive as PTAs because they contain many π electrons and behave like the free electrons of metallic nanomaterials. 18,97 As shown in Fig. 11 , PTT is an oxygen-independent treatment, under red to infrared laser irradiation, CDs are able to convert the absorbed light energy into heat due to their high intrinsic absorbance and selectively accumulate within the tumor through the EPR effect, which kills the heat-insensitive tumor cells.

The second near-infrared (NIR-II) biological window absorption of CDs as PTAs has a strong tissue penetration ability. As shown in Fig. 12 , Tian et al. 98 prepared nickel nitrogen co doped CDs-12 by one pot hydrothermal imaging guided PTT in NIR-II window. CDs-12 had obvious absorption in NIR-II region, and the photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) was as high as 76.1 % at 1064 nm. It shows high-efficiency tumor accumulation ability and can be discharged out of the body through filtration. CDs-12 can be used for photothermal imaging (PTI), photoacoustic imaging (PAI), and T1-weighted MRI, which provide a reference for designing multifunctional photothermal agents.

CDs are used as carriers for PTAs to achieve controlled release under TME stimulation for precise treatment. Choi et al. 99 synthesised pH/redox responsive CDs loaded with IR825 to form CDs-13. As shown in Fig. 13 , CDs-13 entered MDA-MB-231 cells and released IR825 in the presence of low pH and GSH to restore fluorescence. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, IR825 generated heat to induce cell death. This method demonstrates the potential of CDs as a carrier by diagnosing and treating tumors through stimulation by TME.

CDs targeting mitochondria significantly improves the therapeutic effect. Shen et al. 100 obtained a cancer-selective mitochondria-targeted PTT sensitizing nanoagent (CDs-14) by self-assembling small CDs with citric acid and dicyandiamide in an acidic environment, as shown in Fig. 14 . The survival rate difference between HepG2 cells and normal cells was up to 70 %. High temperature can cause structural changes in cell lipids, proteins and DNA, which can induce cell death. This approach of targeting CDs-14 to mitochondria enhances PTT and shows specificity for malignant cells, which provides a subcellular targeting strategy based on CDs.

The stability of the CDs improves the loading rate of the PTAs and enhances the therapeutic effect. As shown in Fig. 15 . Ryplida et al. 101 designed a pH-responsive loading of indocyanine green (ICG) and fluorescent CDs encapsulated in mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) CDs-15 for image-guided PTT. Through zwitterionic CDs and hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions between the ICG, will load to the monodisperse silica, ICG fluorescence light of “off/on” system of temperature control. Acidic pH affects the fluorescence signal of CDs-15, which induces the release of ICG from the stroma and generates heat to kill tumor cells. pH-responsive CDs-15 have potential applications in cancer diagnosis and treatment.

Irradiation of CDs with lower laser power densities reduces damage to healthy tissue. Geng et al. 102 prepared nitrogen-oxygen co-doped CDs-16 using polymers of 1,3,6-nitropyrene (TNP) and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) by one-step molecular fusion method in Fig. 16 . The PCE was 38.3 % and the power density 800 mW/cm2 did not damage other tissues. The tumor destruction effect was 100 %. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, laser energy was converted into heat to kill HeLa cells, which resulted in irreversible cell damage and subsequent tumor destruction. CDs-16 show a low laser power density, which overcome the inherent shortcomings of PTT and point out the direction of future research on PTT.

The PCE and therapeutic efficacy are improved by modifying CDs. However, most of the reported CDs are mainly absorbed in the visible region, and the absorption effect is not obvious in the red to near infrared region, which cannot effectively treat deep-seated tumors. As shown in Table 2 , PTT generally uses high power density and therefore causes harm to humans. The limited energy transfer efficiency of bioluminescence and chemiluminescence affects the therapeutic effect. CDs-12 reduce the laser power density while ensuring PCE, reducing the damage to normal cells and improving the treatment effect.

CDs can be excited under ultrasound as a sonosensitizer. As shown in Fig. 17 , electron-hole pairs on the stimulated CDs can produce ROS, which enhance the killing power of the ultrasound process on tumor cells. Under ultrasonic irradiation, the shell of the CD is impacted by cavitation bubbles that may break down the CDs surface groups and react rapidly with the surrounding environment to produce transient ROS species. 64 Modified CDs can improve the disadvantages of traditional sonosensitizers such as low chemical stability, potential phototoxicity and skin sensitivity.

CDs can be used to load metal ions to deplete GSH and thus improve efficacy. Geng et al. 103 have developed a low-sensitivity, self-quenching Pt-N co-doped CDs fluorescence probe (Pt/N-CDs) to detect the presence of glutathione GSH in cells by inducing self-assembly of Pyridine N-doped CDs with GSH-lysable binding by Pt ion in Fig. 18 . The GSH-depleted P-Pt/N-CDs were then assembled onto n-type Tio2-X nanosheets to build a GSH-responsive PN connection platform (CDs-17) that integrates GSH detection, GSH depletion, and enhanced SDT capabilities. Under the action of ultrasound, 1O2 and
OH were produced, and the loss of GSH also enhanced the production of ROS, thus killing 143B cells. CDs-17 have the ability to enhance SDT and GSH depletion to eradicate malignant tumors, demonstrating the potential of the p-n junction platform as sonosensitizers.

CDs can also be used alone as a sonosensitizer for cancer treatment. Ding et al. 104 formed CDs-18 by N-doped CDs as sonosensitizers and modified with folic acid in Fig. 19 . Compared with traditional sonosensitizers, CDs-18 had superior sonosensitization efficacy of about 3–5 times. Pyrrole N and pyridine N form catalytic centered during the acoustic chemistry of CDs-18. Meanwhile, oxidative stress response of MDA-MB-231 cells was activated by PEX pathway and apoptosis was induced by p53 pathway. CDs-18 contribute to the guidance of carbon-based sonosensitizers with high sound sensitivity and biosafety (Table 3 ).

Fluorescence from CDs allows the detection of GSH depletion in tumors and enables the monitoring of TME. Li et al.
105 generated CDs-19 by combining fluorescent imaging CDs with Fe 3
+. As shown in Fig. 21 , Fe 3
+ reacted with and consumed GSH in CDs-19, restoring the strong blue fluorescence of CDs and achieving fluorescence imaging of GSH depletion in A549 cells. CDs-19 overcome the adverse effects of GSH overexpression in TME, to prevent GSH from blocking CDT and to enable fluorescence imaging of the important physiological process of GSH depletion. To construct a therapeutic drug that can be used for fluorescence imaging guided GSH depletion to improve CDT.

Combining fluorescence imaging and MRI via red CDs onto a single platform allows deeper tissue penetration to detect tumor cells. Wang et al. 106 synthesized CDs with red light by solvothermal one-step method using p-phenylenediamine as carbon source. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) was covalently coupled to the surface of CDs, and then complexed with Fe 2
+ and Gd3+ to obtain functional red CDs (CDs-20). As shown in Fig. 22 , CDs-20 inhibit A549 cells by producing
OH at the tumor site through Fenton reaction, and can be used as H2O2-driven
OH generator for bimodal FLI/MRI. With strong fluorescence and the ability to treat cancer through the Fenton reaction, CDs-20 have promising applications for integrating diagnosis and treatment into a single platform.

Fluorescence of CDs detects wound pH and inhibits tumor recurrence. Zhang et al. 107 designed a multifunctional sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel to fix hemoglobin (Hb) and pH-sensitive fluorescence changing CDs-21 in Fig. 23 . CDs were used for wound pH 5–7 monitoring, SA hydrogel could stop bleeding and Hb could induce Fenton reaction. With Fe 2
+ at the centre of the haemoglobin group, Hb can generate
OH in H2O2 overexpressed TME via the Fenton reaction, which is highly toxic to 4 T1 cells and inhibits local tumor recurrence. CDs-21 can observe the wound status information in real time, inhibit tumor recurrence, and ensure the efficiency of post-operative tumor treatment.

CDs can be used as carriers for drugs containing metal cations due to their large specific surface area. CDT reagents are transported to tumor sites to produce ROS and deplete GSH in tumor cells, thereby reducing the inhibitory effect of TME (Table 4 ). The enhancement or burst of CDs fluorescence by using metal ions allows real-time monitoring of tumor cell apoptosis. C-centered free radicals are more stable in CDT, and endogenous H2O2 and acidic pH do not affect the therapeutic effect.

Anti-cancer drugs are loaded onto CDs by surface function and transported to specific tumor sites. Due to the EPR effect, CDs tend to accumulate in tumours, facilitating the transport of drug-carrying molecules to tumour tissue. In addition, most complexes of CDs with drugs have cleavable chemical bonds that allow for targeted release of the drug in the acidic environment of the tumour site or in response to other stimuli. 55 The method for enhancing the delivery and controlled release at the target site not only reduces damages caused by conventional drugs, but also improves the treatment effect. CDs can also serve as a scaffold to help peptides, proteins and other biological agents enter cells for effective target gene silencing. 18 .

Delaying the rate of drug release can improve the damage caused by CT. Duan et al. 108 combined doxorubicin (DOX) with CDs to CDs-22 through electrostatic interaction, and then coated with bovine serum albumin (BSA) in Fig. 24 . The optimal drug delivery efficiency was 72.9 %. This system avoids in vivo recognition of reticuloendothelial (RES) tissues, which penetrate into blood vessels through EPR effects in tumor tissues. Under acidic conditions, the hydrophobic bond between BSA and CDs-22 was opened, the electrostatic force was protonated and DOX was released from the vector, killing MGC-803 cells. CD-22 reduce damage to the body through the slow release of the drug and this approach is a promising strategy for CT in oncology.

Loading CDs with targeted tumor factors reduces drug leakage, alleviates drug damage to normal tissue and improves the effectiveness of CT. Guo et al. 109 prepared yellow fluorescent CDs nanoclusters loaded with the anticancer drug DOX with 81 % efficiency and encapsulated cancer cell membranes to form CDs-23. As shown in Fig. 25 , CDs-23 kept fluorescence off under normal physiological conditions and selectively turned on fluorescence at GSH overexpressed tumor sites, which dissociated and released DOX to kill B16 cells. CDs-23 enhance deep tumor penetration and enables efficient CT using fluorescence imaging for cancer treatment monitoring.

Loading CDs with multiple drugs inhibits multidrug resistance (MDR) in tumour cells, and thus improves the effectiveness of tumour treatment. Li et al. 110 synthesized a nanodrug based on CDs/dendrimer nanohybrids loaded with polyethylene glycol 1000 vitamin E succinate (TPGS) and DOX dual drug forming CDs-24 by electrostatic interaction in Fig. 26 . The drug delivery efficiency was 40.7 %. DOX was preferentially released in TME, TPGS was also a p-glycoprotein inhibitor, reduced intracellular ATP level and mitochondrial membrane potential, enhanced ROS generation, and significantly inhibited MDR MCF-7/ADR cells. The combination of CDs with dendritic molecules overcomes MDR and enhances CT using the acoustic pore effect, with promising applications in the treatment of other tumor types.

The role of CDs as drug carriers in CT. In the above works in Table 5 , loading the drug onto CDs has excellent structural stability. Delivery to the tumor cells through the acidic pH under the tumor prevents premature release before entering the tumor site. The toxic effect of the anti-cancer drug on normal cells is reduced and the solubility of the drug is improved. However, CDs are readily cleared by plasma and the drug still causes damage to normal cells and the immune system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic methods of CDs for cancer therapy
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-based cancer treatment can significantly improve the limitations of conventional therapeutic approaches. However, the inherent defects of monotherapy are still difficult to overcome holistically, 111 which leaves the elimination of malignant tumors incomplete. Combining multiple monotherapies into a single therapeutic platform through modification of CDs can improve overall therapeutic efficacy, reduce individual drug-related toxicity, and significantly improve treatment outcomes.

CDs can be used as both PSs and PTAs, or as a carrier to participate in treatment, which can reduce the laser power density and alleviate the hypoxic environment of the tumor. Under laser irradiation, CDs generate ROS and heat. ROS can destroy heat shock protein, and the heat generated can increase the concentration of O2, so as to enhance the effect of collaborative therapy.

The use of CDs to enable phototherapy to enter the circulating center of the lysosome can improve the effectiveness of treatment. Zhao et al. 112 synthesized yellow fluorescent CDs-25 by hydrothermal treating trinitropyran in NaOH aqueous solution in Fig. 27 . CDs-25 could be excited by 457 nm and 800 nm lasers and selectively aggregated in lysosomes. The conversion of light energy into heat through laser irradiation, along with the production of 1O2 and
OH, which can not only produce cytotoxic effects through ROS but also dissolve HeLa cells through heat. The PCE of CDs-25 and the QY of 1O2 were 73.5 % and 5.7 %, respectively. Bai et al. 113 synthesized CDs-26 co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen atoms by hydrothermal method. CDs-26 was used for fluorescence imaging of lysosomal and tumor pH, while lethal 1O2 and heat had inhibitory effects on HeLa cells. The efficiency of prepared 1O2 was 27 % and the PCE was 34.4 %. CDs-25 and CDs-26 can selectively accumulate in lysosomes, which disrupt cell viability and stability, and are promising therapeutic agents with synergistic PDT/PTT effects.

Combining immune checkpoint blockade therapy with phototherapy using CDs can enhance anti-tumor immune response. Zhang et al. 114 prepared CDs-27 by integrating positive charge induced cell uptake of polymer-poly (γ-glutamic acid) (γ-PGA) and glucose oxidase (GOx). As shown in Fig. 28 , endogenous H2O2 production induced by CDs-27 alleviated tumor hypoxia and enhanced PDT. Under irradiation at 730 nm, the PCE of 4 T1 cells was 30.3 %. GOx promoted the conversion of glucose, H2O and O2 to gluconate and H2O2, resulting in the formation of 1O2. Checkpoint blockade immunotherapy combined with ligand programmed death ligand1 antibody inhibited the function of cytotoxic T cells. This method is a prospective strategy in oncology treatment by both removing primary tumors and suppressing metastatic tumors.

The power of laser irradiation can be reduced by loading a small amount of PSs onto the photothermal agent and triggering by a single laser. Sun et al. 115 prepared amino-rich red-emitting CDs by solvothermal method and immobilized the photosensitizer Ce6 on the CDs to form CDs-28. As shown in Fig. 29 , CDs-28 can penetrate nuclear pores and selectively gather nucleoli rich in RNA. Irradiation with a 500 mW/cm2 laser at 671 nm was effective in generating 1O2, which increased the cell temperature and promoted PTT. The PCE was 46 %. CDs-28 overcome the inherent disadvantages of PTT and PDT by loading a small amount of PSs onto PTAs as a strategy to improve the effectiveness of cancer phototherapy.

CDs as PSs or PTAs often require different light sources for excitation, whereas PDT and PTT use a single light source, simplifying the treatment process and improving efficiency. Liu et al.
116 used a mixture of CDs, IR820 dye, and an anti-vascular endothelial growth factor adapter as a novel nanomedicine (CDs-29). As shown in Fig. 30 , CDs-29 were irradiated by 808 nm laser and produced PDT I, PDT II and PTT to inhibit tumor cells. In a hypoxic environment,
OH, O2
−, 1O2 and heat could be produced simultaneously through three pathways. CDs-29 trigger all three phototherapeutic pathways simultaneously by single wavelength laser irradiation and remain highly effective in a hypoxic tumor environment. This approach constructs a new protocol to suppress the hypoxic environment.

The application of CDs in collaborative phototherapy was summarized. As a photosensitive molecule in phototherapy, CDs can be irradiated by a single laser with simple installation and short processing time, which can well solve the problem of high-power laser irradiated PTT (Table 6 ). It provides a highly effective cooperative therapy for cancer treatment. In addition to the synergistic treatment of PDT and PTT, other treatments based on CDs can also improve the therapeutic effect through synergistic treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other CDs-based synergistic methods for cancer therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The complexity of TME and the inherent pitfalls of monotherapy can limit the effectiveness of cancer treatment. By specifically selecting precursors, multimodality therapy is integrated into a single platform through CDs, easily reaching deep tissues and removing malignant tumors. The process is simplified to maximize treatment efficacy. This section describes a variety of assisted treatment approaches based on CDs, including PTT/CDT, SDT/CDT, PDT/CT, PTT/CT, CDT/CT, PDT/CDT/CT, PDT/PTT/CDT and PDT/PTT/CT.

CDs can also play the role of a gene carrier to improve the efficiency of gene transfection and enhance synergistic therapeutic effects. Luo et al. 117 prepared CDs-30 in Fig. 31 assembled from iron phthalocyanine and amphiphilic lipopeptides. The introduction of iron-doped CDs into nanohybrid materials could be used for FLI/PAI/PTI/MRI. The PCE was 63.4 %, which significantly improved gene transfection efficiency and peroxisome activity that synergistically inhibited 4 T1 cells. CDs-30 enhance gene therapy through transfection, and open up new avenues of gene delivery as well as PTT/CDT synergistic therapy.

A single ROS type has a limited tumor-killing effect and is influenced by TME. To increase the number of ROS types, Jana et al. 118 synthesized fluorescent hybrid CDs-31 using a mixture of copper acetyl acetone, molybdenum dichloride and IR-780 iodide in Fig. 32 . The redox reaction between copper and molybdenum resulted in the decomposition of H2O2 to
OH within the tumor. CDs-31 in the presence of US produced 1O2 and O2
- via SDT, and cavitation bubbles increased the mass transfer rate of the Fenton reaction, which ablated tumor cells by oxidizing cellular tissue. CDs-31 produce multiple ROS types that disrupt the equilibrium state within tumor cells, and this protocol provides a strategy for mixing CDs in SDT/CDT.

CDs can be used not only as a carrier for drugs, but also as PSs for the direct treatment of cancer. Yao et al. 119 synthesized CDs-32 via CDs and hyaluronic acid, which was then attached to bovine serum albumin and encapsulated DOX and Met in Fig. 33 . Met was released more quickly than DOX, and the total amount of Met released in the first 24 h reached 82.86 %, which was beneficial to improve the hypoxic tumor environment and enhance the efficacy of CT. Red fluorescence is also a tracking tool for deep tissue imaging and, together with PDT, inhibits MCF-7 cells. CDs-32 reduce O2 consumption at the tumor site, improve the efficiency of PDT and CT, which have a broad prospect in clinical application.

The fluorescence of the CDs facilitates visualisation of the drug delivery system. Lu et al.
120 prepared a mesoporous carbon nanocomposite (MCN) coated with double-porous silica to form CDs-33 in Fig. 34 . The PCE was 30.5 % and drug loading efficiency was up to 48.2 %. CDs were covalently attached to the composite surface, preventing premature DOX released and using fluorescence, enabling visualisation of the drug delivery system. Under TME, DOX was rapidly released due to disulfide bond breaking and protonation. The acidic environment and near-infrared radiation also contribute to the synergistic effect of drug release that kills 4 T1 cells. CDs-33 reduce drug damage to the body and use in vivo tracking imaging to enable efficient chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor therapy.

The use of nanocarriers to transport drugs has limitations in terms of complex synthesis and low drug loading capacity. In order to solve this problem, Zhao et al.
121 assembled CDs-34 by the pairing interaction of CDs, DOX and Cu2+, and stabilized it through PVP in Fig. 35 . CDs-34 reacts with GSH and acidic pH in TME, releasing DOX, Cu2+ and CDs. Through the Fenton reaction, H2O2 was catalyzed in the cell to generating
OH. Meanwhile, GSH depletion and DOX promote the production of H2O2, enhancing CDT and CT. CDs-34 are novel carrier-free nanoplatforms that exploit the specific response of TME to achieve synergistic treatment with CDT and CT, this approach opens up new strategies for cancer treatment.

CDs are used as drug carriers to enhance cellular uptake and increase drug utilization. Han et al. 122 constructed a drug delivery system using l-arginine as the precursor through silver and copper co-doping. As shown in Fig 36 , after loading DOX and coating M1 macrophage-derived microvesicles (MMVs), the prepared CDs-35 was highly targeted to the tumor site. Reactive nitrogen can be produced, releasing ROS through CDT, consuming GSH, regulating the band structure of CDs-35 and producing PDT under laser irradiation. DOX could be released through depletion of copper ion, and the cell apoptosis rate was 87.11 % to inhibit 4 T1 cells growth. CDs-35 are involved in the synergistic treatment of PDT/CDT/CT, which demonstrate the potential of CDs as drug delivery carriers by loading drugs with metal ions.

In order to mitigate the damage caused by the drug, TME-responsive CDs need to be prepared for precision cancer therapy. Sun et al.
123 prepared TME stimulus response nanoplatform (CDs-36) by assembling photosensitizer Ce6 and Cu2+ to modify CDs. Cu2+ enhanced the EPR effect in the assembly, burst the fluorescence of CDs and reacted with H2O2 in 4 T1 cells to produce highly toxic
OH, and improved TME by depleting GSH. CDs-36 entered the tumor site and FL visualisation and PDT were restored, which facilitated the treatment of deeper tissues. As shown in Fig. 37 , under laser irradiation, CDs-36 produced 1O2 and thermally inhibited tumor cells.The PCE was 45.7 %. CDs-36 achieve a synergistic PDT/PTT/CDT treatment, building a new platform for imaging-guided therapy.

Exploiting the PDT properties of CDs being used in combination therapies can enhance the overall therapeutic efficacy. Chen et al. 124 loaded PSs CDs, DOX and gold nanoparticles onto porous silicon dioxide carriers and sealed the drug-loading holes with a hyaluronic acid/alginate/chitosan (HCA) multilayer membrane to form CDs-37. As shown in Fig. 38 , CDs and gold nanoparticles generated 1O2 and heat under laser irradiation. The loading of DOX was 18.7 % and drug release was achieved by TME stimulation. This approach proposes a new drug delivery strategy that allows simultaneous PTT, PDT and CT, which expands the potential of CDs in cancer therapy.

Table 7 summarises the synergistic methods of CDs involved in cancer treatment. It can be seen that modified CDs can integrate PDT, PTT, SDT, CDT, CT and other therapeutic approaches into one platform. With excellent photo conversion efficiency and drug loading capacity, while improving the shortcomings of monotherapy and reducing the corresponding side effects, thus enhancing the treatment of deep-seated tumours. In addition, multi-modal imaging of CDs combined with therapeutic techniques can be used for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes, providing accurate metabolic information for the visualisation of drug delivery and therapeutic processes. However, the synthetic routes of these methods are more complex and less stable. However, the synthetic routes of these methods are more complex and less stable.

The physicochemical properties of CDs enable them to bind targeting ligands and chemotherapeutic agents for a wide range of cancer therapeutic applications. This review describes the research progress of CDs-based cancer treatment methods in the last five years. According to the methods used, they are classified into PDT, PTT, SDT, CDT, CT and various synergistic methods including PDT/PTT, CDT/PTT, CDT/SDT, CT/PDT, CT/PTT, CT/CDT, PDT/PTT/CT, PDT/PTT/CDT and CDT/PDT/CT. CDs are applied in the above cancer treatment methods, which can be used as reagents alone or as carriers to carry drugs, avoiding the early release of drugs and reducing damage to normal cells. The preparation of efficient PSs using CDs to improve drug loading efficiency and stability is a major direction of current research. However, the hypoxic environment in tumors limits the effectiveness of CDs-based therapy, leading to the recovery of cancer cells from metastasis. Some studies have exploited the overexpression of GSH, H2O2 and lower pH than normal cells in tumors to generate O2 in situ and alleviate tumor hypoxia, or by adding targeting factors to CDs to shorten the drug transmission distance and improve the therapeutic effect. It is hoped that some insights into the development and future of CDs in cancer therapy can be provided, and novel multifunctional CDs can be designed to solve the above problems and be more widely used in the biomedical field.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jinxia Xu, graduated from Nankai University with a Ph.D in 2010. She is currently a lecturer in Tiangong University. Mainly engaged in the research of fluorescent probe materials and magnetic functional complex materials, exploring new methods for the preparation of nano-diagnostic materials.

Jin Ning, was born in China in 1998 and graduated from Heilongjiang University of Science and Technology with a Bachelor's degree in 2020. He is currently a master student in the School of Chemistry at Tiangong University. His research interests include the design, synthesis and medical applications of carbon dots.

Yao Wang was born in China in 1998 and graduated from Weifang University of Science and Technology with a Bachelor's degree in 2020. She is currently a master's student in the School of Chemical Engineering at Tiangong University. Her research interests include the design and synthesis of carbon dots and photocatalysis.

Ming Xu was born in China in 1998 and graduated with a Bachelor's degree from Zhongyuan University of Technology in 2020. He is currently a master's student in the School of Chemistry at Tiangong University. His research interests are in fluorescent probes and sensors.

Chunhui Yi was born in China in 1997 and graduated from Henan University in 2020 with a Bachelor's degree. She is currently a master's student in the School of Environmental Science and Engineering at Tiangong University. Her research interests include nanomaterials and solid carbon dots.

Fanyong Yan, graduated from Tianjin University in 2007 with a Ph.D. The current professor of Tiangong University. Mainly engaged in the research of functional nanomaterials and applications. In recent years, he has carried out work on the surface reconstruction of nano-optical probes for visual trace analysis, chemical precision measurement and molecular imaging, and the exploration and application of new preparation methods of nano-diagnostic materials.
